name: Big-O: Runtime & Memory Complexity
description: Complexity analysis from scratch: reading the growth class off code, every class from O(1) to O(n!), space complexity, calculation rules, and Big-O vs Theta vs Omega. From the komplexitas tutorial.

topics.name: Big-O Basics & Why It Matters
topics.description: What Big-O measures, the two simplification rules, and common misconceptions.
topics.studyNotes:
An algorithm can be fine at 10 items and collapse at 10 million. Complexity analysis answers one question: how does the running time (and memory) grow as the input size n grows? We do not measure seconds — that depends on the machine, the language, and the load. We measure how many times the work multiplies when the input doubles.

Analogy: if you scan 1 phone-book page per second, 1000 pages take 1000 seconds linearly. But with a halving search (binary search), 1000 pages take about 10 steps. Same task, drastically different scaling — that is what Big-O captures.

Big-O ("ordo") is an upper bound on the growth rate for large n, usually stated for the worst case. It is a notation applied to two independent resources: running time (CPU operations) and memory (space). Every algorithm therefore has two separate complexities, and you state them separately — for example "O(n) time, O(1) space".

Two simplification rules do all the work:
- Drop constant factors: 5n becomes O(n), 3n² becomes O(n²). Whether it is 5× or 1× the work does not change the shape of the growth.
- Keep only the dominant term: n² + 100n + 500 becomes O(n²), because for large n the n² term outruns everything else. At n=5 the terms are comparable; at n=50 the quadratic term is already many times the rest.

Common misconception: Big-O is not the exact running time and does not mean "always slow". An O(n²) function can beat an O(n) one on small inputs if the O(n) one carries heavy constant overhead. Big-O is about scaling behaviour for large n.

In interviews the classic question is "what is the time complexity of this function?" — the skill being tested is reading the growth class straight off the code.

topics.name: Complexity Classes: O(1) to O(n!)
topics.description: The growth classes with recognition patterns and canonical examples, including the two-pointer palindrome case study.
topics.studyNotes:
Speed order, fastest to slowest: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!). The flatter the growth curve, the better it scales. O(1) and O(log n) are nearly flat; O(n²) and especially O(2ⁿ) explode — fine for small n, unusable quickly after.

## O(1) — constant

The running time does not depend on input size: 10 items or 10 million, same number of steps. Typical: array indexing, hash-map get, a single arithmetic operation. An array is contiguous in memory, so element 3's address is computable (base + 3 × element size) — one step regardless of length. Hash-map get is O(1) on average.

## O(log n) — logarithmic (halving)

Each step halves the problem, so the step count is on the order of log₂(n): 1000 items → ~10 steps, 1,000,000 → ~20 steps. Classic example: binary search in a sorted array — inspect the middle, discard the half that cannot contain the target, repeat. 16 elements are found in 4 halvings (log₂ 16 = 4). Recognition: if each step halves or thirds the remaining work instead of moving one-by-one, it is logarithmic. Balanced tree (BST) search and insert are also O(log n).

## O(n) — linear

Work grows in direct proportion to input: double n, double the work. Typical: a single loop over the data — sum, max, search in an unsorted array.

Case study — palindrome check with two pointers: one pointer starts at the front, one at the back, and they compare pairs while moving toward the middle. About n/2 comparisons → O(n) time, and only two index variables → O(1) space. The naive alternative — reverse the string and compare — is also O(n) time but allocates an n-sized copy, so it costs O(n) extra memory. Both are linear in time; they differ in space. Saying this out loud is exactly what interviewers want: complexity has two dimensions, time AND space.

## O(n log n) — linearithmic

The efficient comparison sorts live here: merge sort, quicksort on average, heapsort. Intuition: halving produces log n levels, and each level does n total work (merging) → n × log n. This is the practical speed limit for general-purpose sorting; JS engines' Array.prototype.sort is O(n log n).

## O(n²) — quadratic

The telltale sign: a loop inside a loop, both running to n — every element compared with every other, n × n operations. Examples: bubble sort, naive duplicate search, examining all pairs. n=6 → 36 operations; n=1000 → 1,000,000. The classic optimisation: a duplicate search drops from O(n²) to O(n) by paying O(n) memory for a hash set — the space–time trade-off.

## O(2ⁿ) and O(n!) — exponential and factorial

The work branches at every step and explodes. O(2ⁿ): two branches per element — naive recursive Fibonacci (it recomputes the same subresults over and over), generating all subsets. O(n!): all permutations — brute-force travelling salesman. How bad: 2ⁿ at n=50 is ~10¹⁵ operations (years); 20! is about 2.4×10¹⁸. If you see exponential growth, there is almost always a better approach: memoization, dynamic programming, or a greedy strategy. Memoized Fibonacci is O(n).

## Recognition cheat sheet

- No loop / direct access → O(1)
- Halving each step → O(log n)
- One loop over the input → O(n)
- Sorting or divide-and-conquer → O(n log n)
- Loop inside a loop → O(n²)
- Recursion branching two ways without memoization → O(2ⁿ)

Scale check (operations at n=10 vs n=1000): O(1): 1 → 1; O(log n): ~3 → ~10; O(n): 10 → 1000; O(n log n): ~33 → ~10,000; O(n²): 100 → 1,000,000; O(2ⁿ): ~1024 → astronomical; O(n!): 3,628,800 already at n=10.

topics.name: Space Complexity
topics.description: Measuring extra memory: data structures, the call stack, and why time and space are independent.
topics.studyNotes:
Space complexity measures how much extra memory an algorithm needs beyond the input as n grows. The two main sources are new data structures (arrays, maps, sets) and the recursion call stack.

It uses exactly the same notation as time. It is meaningful to say an algorithm uses O(1), O(n), or O(n²) memory — and the big/little distinction (permissive ≤ vs strict <) applies to space just as it does to time.

The key insight: time and space are independent dimensions of the same algorithm.
- Palindrome, two pointers: O(n) time, O(1) space — just two indices.
- Palindrome, reverse-and-compare: O(n) time, O(n) space — an n-sized copy is created.
- Binary search (iterative): O(log n) time, O(1) space.
- Merge sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) space — the auxiliary merge array.
- Recursive factorial: O(n) time, O(n) space — the call stack.
- Duplicate search with a Set: O(n) time, O(n) space — the set itself.

Rules of thumb:
- O(1) space: a handful of variables regardless of input size (a running sum, two pointers).
- O(n) space: a copy or structure proportional to the input (arr.map(...), a Set holding every element, a reversed string).
- O(recursion depth) space: every recursive call pushes a frame onto the call stack — the depth is the memory. factorial(n) stacks n frames → O(n) space even though it "looks like" it uses no memory. Recurse too deep and you get a stack overflow.

Interview habit: always state both. "O(n) time, O(1) space" is a complete answer; "O(n)" alone is half of one.

topics.name: Calculating Complexity & Asymptotic Notations
topics.description: Practical rules for deriving Big-O from code, plus Big-O vs Theta vs Omega and the little-o/little-omega strict bounds.
topics.studyNotes:
Practical rules for reading complexity off code:
- Sequential steps add up, and the dominant term wins: O(n) + O(n) = O(n); O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²).
- Nested loops multiply: outer n × inner n = O(n²); outer n × inner log n = O(n log n).
- Constant factors drop: O(2n) = O(n); looping over half the array (n/2) is still O(n).
- Halving or thirding inside a loop → log; recursion that halves its input → log levels.
- Separate inputs stay separate: with two different sizes a and b, the answer is O(a + b) for sequential loops or O(a × b) for nested ones — never collapse them into one n and call it O(n²).

Worked example: a function that prints arr[0] (O(1)), then sums the array in one loop (O(n)), then compares all pairs in a double loop (O(n²)) is O(1) + O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²) — the dominant term wins.

## Big-O vs Theta vs Omega

- O (Big-O): upper bound — "the worst case". Unsorted search: O(n), the element is at the end or absent.
- Ω (Omega): lower bound — "the best case". Unsorted search: Ω(1), the element is right at the front.
- Θ (Theta): tight bound — when best and worst are the same order. Unsorted search averages Θ(n).

In practice we almost always quote Big-O because it gives a guarantee. Average case matters when the worst case is misleading — quicksort is the standard example: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst.

## Little-o and little-omega (strict bounds)

Each "big" notation has a strict "little" sibling. The easiest mapping is to number relations:
- O is like ≤ — an upper bound that may be tight: n = O(n) and n = O(n²) are both true.
- o (little-o) is like a strict < — grows strictly slower, the bound is NOT tight: n = o(n²) holds, but n ≠ o(n).
- Ω is like ≥; ω (little-omega) is like a strict >: n² = ω(n).
- Θ is like = — exactly the same order (O and Ω at once).

Intuition for little-o: f = o(g) means f(n)/g(n) → 0 as n → ∞ — g eventually pulls away from f completely. With big-O the two curves may run together forever, which is why n = O(n) is true but n = o(n) is not.

When does this come up? Rarely in practical interviews — big-O is the star. Little-o/little-omega are more theoretical ("this term is o(n), so it is negligible for large n"), but knowing the essence scores points: the big notations are permissive (≤ / ≥), the little ones are strict (< / >).

questions.title: What is Big-O?
questions.prompt: Explain what Big-O notation actually measures and the rules you use to simplify an expression like 3n² + 50n + 200.
questions.answerStructureHint: Define what it measures (growth, not seconds) → the two simplification rules → what it does NOT mean.

expectedPoints.label: Growth rate, not wall-clock time
expectedPoints.description: Measures how work grows with input size n, not seconds — machine and language independent.

expectedPoints.label: Drops constant factors
expectedPoints.description: 5n is O(n); constants do not change the shape of growth.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps only the dominant term
expectedPoints.description: n² + 100n + 500 is O(n²) because n² dominates for large n.

expectedPoints.label: Applies to time AND space
expectedPoints.description: The same notation describes CPU time and memory; an algorithm has both.

followUps.prompt: Can an O(n²) implementation ever be the right choice over an O(n) one?

expectedPoints.label: Small n / constant overhead nuance
expectedPoints.description: For small inputs, constants dominate; O(n²) with tiny constants can win.

questions.title: Why is binary search O(log n)?
questions.prompt: Why is binary search O(log n), and what precondition does it require?

expectedPoints.label: Each step halves the search space
expectedPoints.description: Inspect the middle, discard the half that cannot contain the target.

expectedPoints.label: log₂(n) steps to one element
expectedPoints.description: 1000 elements → ~10 steps, a million → ~20.

expectedPoints.label: Requires sorted input
expectedPoints.description: The halving argument only works on a sorted array.

followUps.prompt: Beyond binary search, how do you recognize O(log n) behaviour in code or data structures?

expectedPoints.label: Recognition pattern
expectedPoints.description: Halving/thirding per step instead of one-by-one; balanced trees (BST) search/insert.

questions.title: Palindrome check: time and space
questions.prompt: Walk me through checking whether a string is a palindrome, and give the time and space complexity of your approach versus the naive reverse-and-compare solution.
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe two pointers → state O(n) time / O(1) space → contrast with reverse-and-compare's O(n) space.

expectedPoints.label: Two-pointer technique
expectedPoints.description: One pointer from the front, one from the back, comparing pairs toward the middle.

expectedPoints.label: O(n) time
expectedPoints.description: About n/2 comparisons; every character touched at most once.

expectedPoints.label: O(1) space vs naive O(n)
expectedPoints.description: Two indices only; reversing creates an n-sized copy.

questions.title: Speeding up an O(n²) duplicate search
questions.prompt: You find a duplicate-detection function with a nested loop comparing every pair. How would you make it faster, and what is the trade-off?

expectedPoints.label: Hash set in one pass
expectedPoints.description: Walk once, add each element to a Set; if already present, it is a duplicate → O(n) time.

expectedPoints.label: Space–time trade-off named
expectedPoints.description: O(n) time is bought with O(n) memory — the classic trade.

expectedPoints.label: Explains why nested loops are O(n²)
expectedPoints.description: Every element compared with every other: n × n operations; n=1000 → a million.

questions.title: Exponential blow-up and how to escape it
questions.prompt: The naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2ⁿ). Explain why, and how you would bring it down.

expectedPoints.label: Two branches per call, recomputing subresults
expectedPoints.description: Each call spawns two more; the same fib(k) values are recomputed over and over.

expectedPoints.label: Memoization / DP fix
expectedPoints.description: Caching subresults makes it O(n); dynamic programming or iteration also works.

expectedPoints.label: Sense of scale
expectedPoints.description: 2ⁿ at n=50 is ~10¹⁵ operations; factorial is even worse (20! ≈ 2.4×10¹⁸).

questions.title: Space complexity and the call stack
questions.prompt: What does space complexity measure, and why does a recursive factorial use O(n) memory even though it allocates no data structures?

expectedPoints.label: Extra memory beyond the input
expectedPoints.description: How auxiliary memory grows with n; main sources are new structures and the call stack.

expectedPoints.label: Recursion depth = stack frames
expectedPoints.description: Every recursive call pushes a frame; factorial(n) stacks n frames → O(n) space, risk of stack overflow.

expectedPoints.label: Time and space are independent
expectedPoints.description: Two O(n)-time algorithms can differ in space (palindrome two-pointer O(1) vs reverse O(n)); state both.

questions.title: Deriving complexity from code
questions.prompt: Give me your practical rules for deriving the Big-O of a function: sequential steps, nested loops, and functions taking two inputs of different sizes.

expectedPoints.label: Sequential steps add, dominant wins
expectedPoints.description: O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²).

expectedPoints.label: Nested loops multiply
expectedPoints.description: Outer n × inner n = O(n²); outer n × inner log n = O(n log n).

expectedPoints.label: Separate inputs stay separate
expectedPoints.description: Two sizes a and b give O(a + b) or O(a × b) — not collapsed into O(n²).

followUps.prompt: What is the difference between Big-O, Theta, and Omega — and when does the average case matter?

expectedPoints.label: O/Θ/Ω distinction
expectedPoints.description: Upper bound (worst) / tight bound / lower bound (best); quicksort as the average-case example.
